title: Persistent Connections, Pipelining, Ordering, Reuse, and Closure · Execution Trace: Idle Timeout Reuse Budget
summary: A server dispatches the next request before consuming the current body, writes responses out of order, reuses connections after framing errors, and waits forever for idle peers. Planned artifact: Add and verify the idle timeout reuse budget increment in persistent-connection request and response state machine. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Bound requests per connection, idle time, lifetime, buffered residual data, and closure reasons without truncating active messages.
Preserve request-response ordering across pipelined HTTP/1.1 messages even when application work finishes out of order.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Idle Timeout Reuse Budget · Theory Idle Timeout Reuse Budget
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 1212 in a archival client parsing incomplete close-delimited responses. Build deterministic Go evidence to preserve HTTP/1.1 response order and reject reuse after incomplete or ambiguous input; preserve this operating constraint: the maintained Go protocol stack remains the production transfer target; then append a second pipelined message and prove the first parser leaves the exact residual bytes. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Bound requests per connection, idle time, lifetime, buffered residual data, and closure reasons without truncating active messages.
